question_html: Show that the function f : R - {3} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R - {1} given by {tex}f(x)=\frac{x-2}{x-3}{/tex} is a bijection.
question_text: Show that the function f : R - {3} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R - {1} given by {tex}f(x)=\frac{x-2}{x-3}{/tex} is a bijection.
explanation: A = R - {3}, B = R - {1} f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B is defined as {tex}f(x)=\left(\frac{x-2}{x-3}\right).{/tex} Let x, y {tex}\in{/tex} A such that f(x) = f(y). {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-2}{x-3}=\frac{y-2}{y-3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2) (y - 3) = (y - 2) (x - 3) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - 3x - 2y + 6 = xy - 3y - 2x + 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3x - 2y = -3y - 2x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x - 2x = 3y - 2y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y Therefore, f is one-one. Let y {tex}\in{/tex} B = R - {1} Then, y {tex}\neq{/tex} 1. The function f is onto if there exists x {tex}\in{/tex} A such that f(x) = y. Now, f (x) = y {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-2}{x-3}=y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2 = xy - 3y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(1 - y) = -3y + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{2-3 y}{1-y} \in \mathbf{A} \quad \quad[y \neq 1]{/tex} Thus, for any y {tex}\in{/tex} B, there exists {tex}\frac{2-3 y}{1-y} \in \mathrm{A}{/tex} such that {tex}f\left(\frac{2-3 y}{1-y}\right)=\frac{\left(\frac{2-3 y}{1-y}\right)-2}{\left(\frac{2-3 y}{1-y}\right)-3}=\frac{2-3 y-2+2 y}{2-3 y-3+3 y}=\frac{-y}{-1}=y{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} f is onto. Hence, function f is one-one and onto.
solution: A = R - {3}, B = R - {1} f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B is defined as {tex}f(x)=\left(\frac{x-2}{x-3}\right).{/tex} Let x, y {tex}\in{/tex} A such that f(x) = f(y). {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-2}{x-3}=\frac{y-2}{y-3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2) (y - 3) = (y - 2) (x - 3) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - 3x - 2y + 6 = xy - 3y - 2x + 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3x - 2y = -3y - 2x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x - 2x = 3y - 2y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y Therefore, f is one-one. Let y {tex}\in{/tex} B = R - {1} Then, y {tex}\neq{/tex} 1. The function f is onto if there exists x {tex}\in{/tex} A such that f(x) = y. Now, f (x) = y {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-2}{x-3}=y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2 = xy - 3y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(1 - y) = -3y + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{2-3 y}{1-y} \in \mathbf{A} \quad \quad[y \neq 1]{/tex} Thus, for any y {tex}\in{/tex} B, there exists {tex}\frac{2-3 y}{1-y} \in \mathrm{A}{/tex} such that {tex}f\left(\frac{2-3 y}{1-y}\right)=\frac{\left(\frac{2-3 y}{1-y}\right)-2}{\left(\frac{2-3 y}{1-y}\right)-3}=\frac{2-3 y-2+2 y}{2-3 y-3+3 y}=\frac{-y}{-1}=y{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} f is onto. Hence, function f is one-one and onto.
solution_html:
A = R - {3}, B = R - {1}
f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B is defined as {tex}f(x)=\left(\frac{x-2}{x-3}\right).{/tex}
Let x, y {tex}\in{/tex} A such that f(x) = f(y).
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-2}{x-3}=\frac{y-2}{y-3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2) (y - 3) = (y - 2) (x - 3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - 3x - 2y + 6 = xy - 3y - 2x + 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -3x - 2y = -3y - 2x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x - 2x = 3y - 2y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y
Therefore, f is one-one.
Let y {tex}\in{/tex} B = R - {1}
Then, y {tex}\neq{/tex} 1.
The function f is onto if there exists x {tex}\in{/tex} A such that f(x) = y.
Now, f (x) = y
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-2}{x-3}=y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2 = xy - 3y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(1 - y) = -3y + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{2-3 y}{1-y} \in \mathbf{A} \quad \quad[y \neq 1]{/tex}
Thus, for any y {tex}\in{/tex} B, there exists {tex}\frac{2-3 y}{1-y} \in \mathrm{A}{/tex} such that
{tex}f\left(\frac{2-3 y}{1-y}\right)=\frac{\left(\frac{2-3 y}{1-y}\right)-2}{\left(\frac{2-3 y}{1-y}\right)-3}=\frac{2-3 y-2+2 y}{2-3 y-3+3 y}=\frac{-y}{-1}=y{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} f is onto.
Hence, function f is one-one and onto.

question_html: Let f : N {tex}\to{/tex} N be defined by {tex}f(n)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {n+1, \text { if } n \text { is odd }} \\ {n-1, \text { if } n \text { is even }} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Show that f is a bijection.
question_text: Let f : N {tex}\to{/tex} N be defined by {tex}f(n)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {n+1, \text { if } n \text { is odd }} \\ {n-1, \text { if } n \text { is even }} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Show that f is a bijection.
explanation: We have, {tex}f(n)=\left\{\begin{array}{lll} {n+1} & {\text { if }} & {n \text { is odd }} \\ {n-1} & {\text { if }} & {n \text { is even }} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Injectivity : Case I: If n is odd, Let x, y {tex}\in{/tex} N such that f (x)=f (y) As, f (x) = f (y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 1 = y + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y Case II: If n is even, Let x, y {tex}\in{/tex} N such that f (x)=f (y) As, f (x) = f (y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 1 = y - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y Therefore, f is injective. Surjectivity: Case I: If n is odd, As, for every n {tex}\in{/tex} N, there exists y = n - 1 in N such that f (y) = f(n - 1) = n - 1 + 1 = n Case II: If n is even, As, for every n {tex}\in{/tex} N, there exists y = n + 1 in N such that f (y) = f (n + 1) = n + 1 - 1 = n Therefore, f is surjective. Hence, f is a bijection
solution: We have, {tex}f(n)=\left\{\begin{array}{lll} {n+1} & {\text { if }} & {n \text { is odd }} \\ {n-1} & {\text { if }} & {n \text { is even }} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Injectivity : Case I: If n is odd, Let x, y {tex}\in{/tex} N such that f (x)=f (y) As, f (x) = f (y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 1 = y + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y Case II: If n is even, Let x, y {tex}\in{/tex} N such that f (x)=f (y) As, f (x) = f (y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 1 = y - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y Therefore, f is injective. Surjectivity: Case I: If n is odd, As, for every n {tex}\in{/tex} N, there exists y = n - 1 in N such that f (y) = f(n - 1) = n - 1 + 1 = n Case II: If n is even, As, for every n {tex}\in{/tex} N, there exists y = n + 1 in N such that f (y) = f (n + 1) = n + 1 - 1 = n Therefore, f is surjective. Hence, f is a bijection
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}f(n)=\left\{\begin{array}{lll} {n+1} & {\text { if }} & {n \text { is odd }} \\ {n-1} & {\text { if }} & {n \text { is even }} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Injectivity :
Case I: If n is odd,
Let x, y {tex}\in{/tex} N such that f (x)=f (y)
As, f (x) = f (y)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 1 = y + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y
Case II: If n is even,
Let x, y {tex}\in{/tex} N such that f (x)=f (y)
As, f (x) = f (y)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 1 = y - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y
Therefore, f is injective.
Surjectivity:
Case I: If n is odd,
As, for every n {tex}\in{/tex} N, there exists y = n - 1 in N such that
f (y) = f(n - 1) = n - 1 + 1 = n
Case II: If n is even,
As, for every n {tex}\in{/tex} N, there exists y = n + 1 in N such that f (y) = f (n + 1) = n + 1 - 1 = n
Therefore, f is surjective.
Hence, f is a bijection

question_html: Show that the function f :N {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} N, defined by {tex}f(n)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} \frac{n+1}{2}, \text { if } n \text { is odd } \\ \frac{n}{2}, \text { if } n \text { is even } \end{array}\right.{/tex} is one one and onto function.
question_text: Show that the function f :N {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} N, defined by {tex}f(n)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} \frac{n+1}{2}, \text { if } n \text { is odd } \\ \frac{n}{2}, \text { if } n \text { is even } \end{array}\right.{/tex} is one one and onto function.
explanation: We have, {tex}f(1)=\frac{(1+1)}{2}=\frac{2}{2}=1 \quad \text { and } \quad f(2)=\frac{2}{2}=1{/tex} Thus, f(1) = f(2) while 1 = 2 f is many-one, In order to show that f is onto, consider an arbitrary element if n is odd then ( 2n - 1 ) is odd, and {tex}f(2 n-1)=\frac{(2 n-1+1)}{2}=\frac{2 n}{2}=n{/tex} If n is even then 2n is even and {tex}f(2 n)=\frac{2 n}{2}=n{/tex} Thus, for each n {tex} \in {/tex} N whether even or odd there exists its pre image in N f is onto Hence, f is many-one onto.

Thus, f(1) = f(2) while 1 = 2
f is many-one,
In order to show that f is onto, consider an arbitrary element 
if n is odd then ( 2n - 1 ) is odd, and 
{tex}f(2 n-1)=\frac{(2 n-1+1)}{2}=\frac{2 n}{2}=n{/tex}
If n is even then 2n is even and 
{tex}f(2 n)=\frac{2 n}{2}=n{/tex}
Thus, for each n {tex} \in {/tex} N whether even or odd there exists its pre image in N 
f is onto 
Hence, f is many-one onto.

question_text: If A = { 1, 2, 3}, B = { 4, 5, 6, 7} and let f = {(1, 4), (2, 5), (3, 6)} be a function from A to B. Show that f is one-one.
explanation: Given, A = {1, 2, 3}, B = {4, 5, 6, 7} and f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B is defined as f = {(1, 4), (2, 5), (3, 6)} i.e. f(1) = 4, f(2) = 5 and f(3) = 6. It can be seen that the images of distinct elements of A under f are distinct. So, f is one-one.In other words, no two elements of set A are associated with set B which implies that there is one to one correspondence between X and Y.

question_text: State the reason for the relation R in the set {1, 2, 3} given by R = {(1, 2), (2, 1)} not to be transitive.
explanation: The given relation R in the set {1, 2, 3} is given by R = {(1, 2), (2, 1)} We know that, for a relation to be transitive, (x,y) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (y,z) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R. Here, (1, 2) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (2, 1) {tex}\in{/tex} R but (1, 1) {tex}\notin{/tex} R. Therefore, R is not transitive.

but (1, 1) {tex}\notin{/tex} R.

question_html: If f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R is the function defined by f(x) = 4x3 + 7, then show that f is a bijection.
question_text: If f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R is the function defined by f(x) = 4x 3 + 7, then show that f is a bijection.
explanation: We are given that f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R is the function defined by f(x) = 4x 3 + 7, then we need to show that f is a bijection. The given function is f : R {tex} \to{/tex} R such that f(x) = 4x 3 + 7 To show f is bijective, we are required to show that f is one-one as well as onto. One-One function Now,let x 1 , x 2 {tex} \in{/tex} R such that f(x 1 ) = f(x 2 ), {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 4x 1 3 + 7 = 4x 2 3 + 7 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 4x 1 3 = 4x 2 3 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 3 - x 2 3 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 1 - x 2 )(x 1 2 + x 1 x 2 + x 2 2 )= 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( x _ { 1 } - x _ { 2 } \right) \left[ \left( x _ { 1 } + \frac { x _ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 4 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Either x 1 - x 2 = 0 ...(i) or, {tex} \left[ \left( x _ { 1 } + \frac { x _ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 4 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \right] = 0{/tex} ...(ii) But, Eq. (ii) is not possible as x 1 , x 2 {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\therefore{/tex} x 1 - x 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = x 2 Thus, f(x 1 ) = f(x 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = x 2 , {tex}\forall{/tex} x 1 , x 2 {tex}\in{/tex} R Therefore, f(x) is a one-one function. Onto function Now,let y {tex}\in{/tex} R ( codomain) be any arbitrary number. Then, f(x) = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x 3 + 7 = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x 3 = y - 7 which is a real number. [ {tex}\because{/tex} y {tex}\in{/tex} R] Thus, for every y {tex}\in{/tex} R(codomain), there exists {tex}x = \left( \frac { y - 7 } { 4 } \right) ^ { 1 / 3 } \in R{/tex} (domain) such that {tex}f ( x ) = f \left[ \left( \frac { y - 7 } { 4 } \right) ^ { 1 / 3 } \right]{/tex} {tex} = 4 \left[ \left( \frac { y - 7 } { 4 } \right) ^ { 1 / 3 } \right] ^ { 3 } + 7{/tex} {tex}= 4 \left( \frac { y - 7 } { 4 } \right){/tex} + 7 = y - 7 + 7 = y Thus,f(x) is an onto function.Thus, we arrived at the conclusion that f(x) is both one-one and onto, hence, it is a bijective.

(ii) is not possible as x 1 , x 2 {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\therefore{/tex} x 1 - x 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = x 2 Thus, f(x 1 ) = f(x 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = x 2 , {tex}\forall{/tex} x 1 , x 2 {tex}\in{/tex} R Therefore, f(x) is a one-one function. Onto function Now,let y {tex}\in{/tex} R ( codomain) be any arbitrary number. Then, f(x) = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x 3 + 7 = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x 3 = y - 7 which is a real number. [ {tex}\because{/tex} y {tex}\in{/tex} R] Thus, for every y {tex}\in{/tex} R(codomain), there exists {tex}x = \left( \frac { y - 7 } { 4 } \right) ^ { 1 / 3 } \in R{/tex} (domain) such that {tex}f ( x ) = f \left[ \left( \frac { y - 7 } { 4 } \right) ^ { 1 / 3 } \right]{/tex} {tex} = 4 \left[ \left( \frac { y - 7 } { 4 } \right) ^ { 1 / 3 } \right] ^ { 3 } + 7{/tex} {tex}= 4 \left( \frac { y - 7 } { 4 } \right){/tex} + 7 = y - 7 + 7 = y Thus,f(x) is an onto function.Thus, we arrived at the conclusion that f(x) is both one-one and onto, hence, it is a bijective.
solution_html:
We are given that f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R is the function defined by f(x) = 4x3 + 7, then we need to show that f is a bijection.
The given function is f : R {tex} \to{/tex} R such that f(x) = 4x3 + 7
To show f is bijective, we are required to show that f is one-one as well as onto.
One-One function
Now,let x1, x2 {tex} \in{/tex} R such that f(x1) = f(x2),
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 4x13 + 7 = 4x23 + 7
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 4x13 = 4x23
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x13 - x23 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x1 - x2)(x12 + x1x2 + x22)= 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left( x _ { 1 } - x _ { 2 } \right) \left[ \left( x _ { 1 } + \frac { x _ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 4 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Either x1 - x2 = 0 ...(i)
or, {tex} \left[ \left( x _ { 1 } + \frac { x _ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 4 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \right] = 0{/tex} ...(ii)
But, Eq. (ii) is not possible as x1, x2 {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x1 - x2 = 0{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}x1 = x2
Thus, f(x1) = f(x2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = x2, {tex}\forall{/tex} x1, x2 {tex}\in{/tex} R
Therefore, f(x) is a one-one function.
Onto function
Now,let y {tex}\in{/tex} R ( codomain) be any arbitrary number.
Then, f(x) = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x3 + 7 = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x3 = y - 7 which is a real number. [{tex}\because{/tex}y {tex}\in{/tex} R]
Thus, for every y {tex}\in{/tex} R(codomain), there exists
{tex}x = \left( \frac { y - 7 } { 4 } \right) ^ { 1 / 3 } \in R{/tex} (domain) such that
{tex}f ( x ) = f \left[ \left( \frac { y - 7 } { 4 } \right) ^ { 1 / 3 } \right]{/tex}{tex} = 4 \left[ \left( \frac { y - 7 } { 4 } \right) ^ { 1 / 3 } \right] ^ { 3 } + 7{/tex}
{tex}= 4 \left( \frac { y - 7 } { 4 } \right){/tex}+ 7 = y - 7 + 7 = y
Thus,f(x) is an onto function.Thus, we arrived at the conclusion that f(x) is both one-one and onto, hence, it is a bijective.

question_html: Show that the relation S in the set R of real numbers defined as S = {(a, b): a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Rand a {tex}\le{/tex} b3} is neither reﬂexive nor symmetric nor transitive.
question_text: Show that the relation S in the set R of real numbers defined as S = {(a, b): a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Rand a {tex}\le{/tex} b 3 } is neither reﬂexive nor symmetric nor transitive.
explanation: We have to Show that the relation S in the set R of real numbers defined as S = {(a, b): a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Rand a {tex}\le{/tex} b 3 } is neither reﬂexive nor symmetric nor transitive. Reﬂexive : As {tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 } \leq \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 }{/tex} where {tex} \frac 12 \in{/tex} R, is not true. {tex} \therefore \quad \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } , \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \notin S{/tex} Thus, S is not reﬂexive. Symmetric: As -2 {tex}\leq{/tex} (3) 3 , where -2, 3 {tex}\in{/tex} R is true but 3 {tex}\leq{/tex} (-2) 3 is not true, i.e. (- 2, 3) {tex}\in{/tex} S but (3, - 2) {tex}\in{/tex} S. Therefore, S is not symmetric. Transitive : As {tex}3 \leq \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { 3 } { 2 } \leq \left( \frac { 4 } { 3 } \right) ^ { 3 }{/tex} where 3, {tex}\frac { 3 } { 2 } , \frac { 4 } { 3 } \in R{/tex} are true but {tex}3 \leq \left( \frac { 4 } { 3 } \right) ^ { 3 }{/tex} is not true. i.e., {tex}\left( 3 , \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right) \in{/tex} S and {tex}\left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } , \frac { 4 } { 3 } \right) \in S{/tex} but {tex}\left( 3 , \frac { 4 } { 3 } \right) \in S{/tex} Therefore, S is not transitive. question_html: Show that the relation S in set A= {x {tex}\in{/tex} Z : 0{tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex}12} given by S = {(a, b): a, b {tex}\in{/tex} A, Ia - bl is divisible by 4} is an equivalence relation. Find the set of all elements related to 1.
question_text: Show that the relation S in set A= {x {tex}\in{/tex} Z : 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 12} given by S = {(a, b): a, b {tex}\in{/tex} A, Ia - bl is divisible by 4} is an equivalence relation. Find the set of all elements related to 1.
explanation: Given relation is S = {(a, b ) : la - bl is divisible by 4 and a, b {tex}\in{/tex} A} and A = {x : x {tex}\in{/tex} Z and 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 12} Now, A can be written as A = {0, 1, 2, 3, . . . , 12} Reﬂexive As for any x {tex}\in{/tex} A we get Ix - xI = 0, which is divisible by 4. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, x) {tex}\in{/tex} S, {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} A Therefore, S is reﬂexive. Symmetric As for any (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} S, we get Ix - yI is divisible by 4.[by using definition of given relation] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I x - y I = 4 {tex}\lambda{/tex} , for some {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} Z {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I y - x I = 4 {tex}\lambda{/tex} , for some {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} Z (y, x) {tex}\in{/tex} S Thus. (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} S =(y, x) {tex}\in{/tex} S , {tex}\forall{/tex} x, y {tex}\in{/tex} A Therefore, S is symmetric Transitive For any (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} S and (y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} S, we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ix - yI is divisible by 4 and {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Iy - z] is divisible by 4. [by using definition of given relation] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ix - yI = 4 {tex}\lambda{/tex} and ly- z| = 4µ, for some {tex}\lambda{/tex} ,µ {tex}\in{/tex} Z. Now, x - z = (x - y) + (y - z) = ± 4 {tex}\lambda{/tex} ± 4µ = ± 4 ( {tex}\lambda{/tex} + µ ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ix - zI is divisible by 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ( x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} S Thus, (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} S and (y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} S {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} S, {tex}\forall{/tex} x, y, z {tex}\in{/tex} A Therefore, S is transitive. Since, S is reflexive, symmetric and transitive, so it is an equivalence relation. Since 1-1=0 which is divisible by 4, therefore 1 is related to 1. Also,5-1=4 which is divisible by 4,therefore 1 is related to 5. Also,9-1=8 which is divisible by 4, therefore 1 is related to 9. Now, set of all elements related to 1 is {1, 5, 9}.

Given relation is S = {(a, b ) : la - bl is divisible by 4 and a, b {tex}\in{/tex} A}
and A = {x : x{tex}\in{/tex}Z and 0{tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 12}
Now, A can be written as A = {0, 1, 2, 3, . . . , 12}
Reﬂexive As for any x{tex}\in{/tex}A we get Ix - xI = 0, which is divisible by 4.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, x){tex}\in{/tex}S, {tex}\forall{/tex} x{tex}\in{/tex}A
 Therefore, S is reﬂexive.
Symmetric As for any (x, y){tex}\in{/tex}S, we get Ix - yI is divisible by 4.[by using definition of given relation]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}I x - y I = 4{tex}\lambda{/tex}, for some {tex}\lambda{/tex}{tex}\in{/tex}Z
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}I y - x I = 4{tex}\lambda{/tex} , for some {tex}\lambda{/tex}{tex}\in{/tex}Z
(y, x){tex}\in{/tex}S
Thus. (x, y){tex}\in{/tex}S =(y, x){tex}\in{/tex}S , {tex}\forall{/tex}x, y {tex}\in{/tex} A
Therefore, S is symmetric
Transitive For any (x, y){tex}\in{/tex}S and (y, z){tex}\in{/tex}S, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Ix - yI is divisible by 4 and
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Iy - z] is divisible by 4. [by using definition of given relation]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ix - yI = 4{tex}\lambda{/tex} and ly- z| = 4µ,
for some {tex}\lambda{/tex},µ{tex}\in{/tex}Z.
Now, x - z = (x - y) + (y - z)
= ± 4{tex}\lambda{/tex} ± 4µ
= ± 4 ( {tex}\lambda{/tex} + µ )
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ix - zI is divisible by 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ( x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} S
Thus, (x, y){tex}\in{/tex}S and (y, z){tex}\in{/tex}S
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex}S, {tex}\forall{/tex}x, y, z{tex}\in{/tex}A
Therefore, S is transitive.
Since, S is reflexive, symmetric and transitive, so it is an equivalence relation.

Since 1-1=0 which is divisible by 4, therefore 1 is related to 1. Also,5-1=4  which is divisible by 4,therefore 1 is related to 5.

Also,9-1=8 which is divisible by 4, therefore 1 is related to 9.

Now, set of all elements related to 1 is {1, 5, 9}.

question_html: Prove that every identity relation on a set is reflexive, but the converse is not necessarily true.
question_text: Prove that every identity relation on a set is reflexive, but the converse is not necessarily true.
explanation: Let A be a set. Then l A = {(a, a) ; a {tex}\in{/tex} A} is the identity realtion on A. Hence, every identity relation on a set is reflexive by definition. Converse: Let A = {(a, b, c)} be a set. Let R = {(a, a) (b, b) (c, c) (a, b)} be a relation defined on A. Clearly R is reflexive on set A, but it is not identity relation on set A as (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R Hence, a reflexive relation need not be identity relation.
solution: Let A be a set. Then l A = {(a, a) ; a {tex}\in{/tex} A} is the identity realtion on A. Hence, every identity relation on a set is reflexive by definition. Let A be a set.

Clearly R is reflexive on set A, but it is not identity relation on set A as (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex}R
Hence, a reflexive relation need not be identity relation.

question_html: Let f: X {tex}\to{/tex} Y is a function. Define a relation R in X given by R = {(a, b): f(a) = f(b)}. Examine whether R is an equivalence relation or not.
question_text: Let f: X {tex}\to{/tex} Y is a function. Define a relation R in X given by R = {(a, b): f(a) = f(b)}. Examine whether R is an equivalence relation or not.
explanation: The given function is f : X {tex}\to{/tex} Y and relation on X is R = {(a, b) : f (a) = f (b)} Reﬂexive Since, for every x {tex}\in{/tex} X, we have {tex}f(x) = f(x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(x, x){/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} X {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is reflexive. Symmetric Let (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R Then, {tex}f(x)=f(y){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(y) = f(x) {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (y, x){/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is symmetric. Transitive Let x, y, z {tex}\in{/tex} X such that (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} Rand (y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R Then {tex}f(x) = f(y){/tex} ........(i) and {tex}f (y) = f (z){/tex} .......(ii) From Equation (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f(x) = f(z){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R Thus, (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} x, y, z {tex}\in{/tex} X {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is transitive. Therefore, R is transitive. Since, R is reﬂexive, symmetric and transitive, so it is an equivalence relation.
solution: The given function is f : X {tex}\to{/tex} Y and relation on X is R = {(a, b) : f (a) = f (b)} Reﬂexive Since, for every x {tex}\in{/tex} X, we have {tex}f(x) = f(x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(x, x){/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} X {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is reflexive. Symmetric Let (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R Then, {tex}f(x)=f(y){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(y) = f(x) {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (y, x){/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is symmetric. Transitive Let x, y, z {tex}\in{/tex} X such that (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} Rand (y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R Then {tex}f(x) = f(y){/tex} ........(i) and {tex}f (y) = f (z){/tex} .......(ii) From Equation (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f(x) = f(z){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R Thus, (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} x, y, z {tex}\in{/tex} X {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is transitive. Therefore, R is transitive. Since, R is reﬂexive, symmetric and transitive, so it is an equivalence relation.
solution_html:
The given function is f : X {tex}\to{/tex} Y and relation on X is R = {(a, b) : f (a) = f (b)}
Reﬂexive Since, for every x{tex}\in{/tex}X, we have
{tex}f(x) = f(x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(x, x){/tex}{tex}\in{/tex}R {tex}\forall{/tex} x{tex}\in{/tex}X
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R is reflexive.

Symmetric Let (x, y){tex}\in{/tex}R
Then, {tex}f(x)=f(y){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(y) = f(x) {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow (y, x){/tex}{tex}\in{/tex}R
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R is symmetric.

Transitive Let x, y, z{tex}\in{/tex}X such that (x, y){tex}\in{/tex}Rand (y, z){tex}\in{/tex}R
Then {tex}f(x) = f(y){/tex}........(i)
and {tex}f (y) = f (z){/tex}.......(ii)
From Equation (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}f(x) = f(z){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R
Thus, (x, y){tex}\in{/tex}R and (y, z){tex}\in{/tex}R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} x, y, z{tex}\in{/tex}X
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R is transitive.
Therefore, R is transitive. Since, R is reﬂexive, symmetric and transitive, so it is an equivalence relation.

(a, b) R (c, d) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}a + d = b + c    where a, b, c, d {tex}\in{/tex}N
(a, b ) R (a, b) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}a + b = a + b      for all (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex}N {tex}\times{/tex}N

Now
(a, b) R (c, d) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + d = b + c   for (a, b ) (c, d) {tex}\in{/tex}N {tex}\times{/tex}N
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}d + a = c + b

Hence R is symmetric.

for (a, b), (c, d) {tex}\in{/tex}N {tex}\times{/tex}N

for(c, d), (e, f) {tex}\in{/tex}N {tex}\times{/tex}N

a + f = b+ e
(a, b) R (e, f)

question_text: Show that the relation R in the set R of real numbers, defined as R = {(a, b): a {tex}\leq{/tex} b 2 } is neither reﬂexive nor symmetric nor transitive.
explanation: R = {(a, b): a {tex}\leq{/tex} b 2 } It can be observed that ( {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ) {tex}\in{/tex} R, since {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} > ( {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ) 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is not reflexive. subject: Mathematics
question_html: Prove that the relation R in set A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} given by R = {(a, b) : Ia - bl is even} is an equivalence relation.
question_text: Prove that the relation R in set A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} given by R = {(a, b) : Ia - bl is even} is an equivalence relation.
explanation: The given relation is R = {(a, b) : {tex}|a - b|{/tex} is even} defined on set A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}. Reﬂexive As {tex}|x - x|{/tex} = 0 is even, {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} A. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, x) {tex}\in{/tex} R, {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is reﬂexive. Symmetric Let (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R = Ix - yI is even [by the definition of relation] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|y - x|{/tex} is also even. [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}|a| = |-a|{/tex} , {tex}\forall{/tex} a {tex}\in{/tex} R] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y, x) {tex}\in{/tex} R Thus, (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y, x) {tex}\in{/tex} R, {tex}\forall{/tex} x, y {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is symmetric. Transitive Let (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|x - y|{/tex} is even and {tex}|y - z|{/tex} is even. [by using.definition of relation] Now, {tex}|x - y|{/tex} is even {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x and y both are even or odd. and {tex}|y - z|{/tex} is even. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y and z both are even or odd. Clearly, two cases arises Case I When y is even. Then, both x and z are even. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|x - z|{/tex} is even. [ {tex}\because{/tex} difference of two even numbers is even] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R Case II When y is odd. Then, both x and z are odd = {tex}|x - z|{/tex} is even. [ {tex}\because{/tex} difference of two odd numbers is even] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R Thus, (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R , {tex}\forall{/tex} x, y, z {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\therefore{/tex} R is transitive. Since, R is reﬂexive, symmetric and transitive, so it is an equivalence relation.

solution_html:
The given relation is R = {(a, b) : {tex}|a - b|{/tex} is even} defined on set A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}.
Reﬂexive

As {tex}|x - x|{/tex} = 0 is even, {tex}\forall{/tex} x{tex}\in{/tex}A.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, x){tex}\in{/tex}R,{tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} A
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R is reﬂexive.
Symmetric Let (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R = Ix - yI is even [by the definition of relation]
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|y - x|{/tex}is also even. [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}|a| = |-a|{/tex} ,{tex}\forall{/tex}a{tex}\in{/tex}R]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y, x){tex}\in{/tex}R
Thus, (x, y){tex}\in{/tex}R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y, x) {tex}\in{/tex} R,{tex}\forall{/tex} x, y{tex}\in{/tex}A
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R is symmetric.
Transitive Let (x, y){tex}\in{/tex} R and (y, z){tex}\in{/tex}R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|x - y|{/tex} is even and {tex}|y - z|{/tex} is even. [by using.definition of relation]
Now, {tex}|x - y|{/tex} is even 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x and y both are even or odd.
and {tex}|y - z|{/tex} is even.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y and z both are even or odd.
Clearly, two cases arises
Case I When y is even. Then, both x and z are even.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|x - z|{/tex} is even. [{tex}\because{/tex} difference of two even numbers is even]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R
Case II When y is odd.
Then, both x and z are odd = {tex}|x - z|{/tex}is even. [{tex}\because{/tex}difference of two odd numbers is even]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R
Thus, (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} R and (y, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, z) {tex}\in{/tex} R , {tex}\forall{/tex} x, y, z {tex}\in{/tex} A
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R is transitive.
Since, R is reﬂexive, symmetric and transitive, so it is an equivalence relation.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Let S be the set of all real numbers. Show that the relation R = {(a, b): a2 + b2 = 1} is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive.
question_text: Let S be the set of all real numbers. Show that the relation R = {(a, b): a 2 + b 2 = 1} is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive.
solution_html:
Given that, {tex}\forall{/tex} a, b {tex}\in {/tex} S, R = {(a, b) : a2 + b2 = 1 }
Now,
R is Reflexive if (a,a) {tex}\in {/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} a {tex}\in {/tex} S
For any a {tex}\in {/tex} S, we have
a2+a2 = 2 a2 {tex}\ne{/tex} 1
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a,a) {tex}\notin {/tex} R
Thus, R is not reflexive.
R is Symmetric:
 if (a,b) {tex}\in {/tex} R = (b,a) {tex}\in {/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} a,b {tex}\in {/tex} S
(a,b) {tex}\in {/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 + a2 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b,a) {tex}\in {/tex} R
Thus, R is symmetric .
R is Transitive:
if (a,b) {tex}\in {/tex} R and (b,c) {tex}\in {/tex} R 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}(a,c) {tex}\in {/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} a,b,c {tex}\in {/tex} S
Let (a,b) {tex}\in {/tex} R and (b,c) {tex}\in {/tex} R {tex}\forall{/tex} a, b,c {tex}\in {/tex} S
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 = 1 and b2 + c2 = 1
Adding both, we get
a2+ c2+2b2 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2+ c2= 2-2b2 {tex}\ne{/tex} 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a, c) {tex}\in {/tex}R
Thus, R is not transitive.
Thus, R is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive
